edges.description: The recurrent linker-region substitutions change how FGFR2 engages FGF ligands.
edges.target: Altered FGFR2 ligand affinity and specificity

edges.description: Altered ligand affinity and specificity permit persistent local receptor activation in Apert osteoblast-lineage cells.
edges.source: Altered FGFR2 ligand affinity and specificity
edges.target: FGF2 autocrine loop and constitutive FGFR2 activation

edges.description: Constitutively active Apert-mutant FGFR2 engages ERK1/2 signaling in mesenchymal cells.
edges.source: FGF2 autocrine loop and constitutive FGFR2 activation

edges.description: Apert-mutant FGFR2 additionally engages a mutation-specific PKCalpha signaling branch.
edges.source: FGF2 autocrine loop and constitutive FGFR2 activation

edges.description: Persistent FGFR2 activation perturbs appendicular skeletal development in Apert syndrome.
edges.source: FGF2 autocrine loop and constitutive FGFR2 activation

edges.description: ERK1/2 signaling contributes to the osteogenic response induced by FGFR2 activation.
edges.target: Enhanced osteoblast differentiation and matrix mineralization in cranial suture mesenchyme

edges.description: PKCalpha signaling mediates the mutant-specific osteogenic differentiation program.
edges.target: Enhanced osteoblast differentiation and matrix mineralization in cranial suture mesenchyme

edges.description: Accelerated osteogenesis in the coronal suture promotes early suture closure.
edges.source: Enhanced osteoblast differentiation and matrix mineralization in cranial suture mesenchyme

edges.description: Early coronal suture closure manifests clinically as coronal craniosynostosis.
Coronal suture fusion restricts normal skull-base growth and craniofacial projection.
Restricted skull-base growth contributes to midface hypoplasia and retrusion.
Skull-base and midfacial growth restriction contributes to shallow orbits and ocular prominence.
Digital morphogenesis failure produces the classic complex hand syndactyly phenotype.
The same appendicular developmental defect produces symmetric toe syndactyly.
Apert hand patterning defects extend to the thumb ray and first web space.
Thumb phalangeal maldevelopment manifests clinically as broad, radially deviated thumbs.

nodes.description: Apert thumbs develop a shortened, broadened, radially deviated proximal phalanx with associated first-webspace deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: The anatomy and management of the thumb in Apert syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The characteristic "hitchhiker" posture or radial clinodactyly of these short but broad digits is caused by an abnormal proximal phalanx.

nodes.description: The S252W and P253R FGFR2 mutations create gain-of-function receptor states with increased FGF affinity and altered ligand specificity, enabling inappropriate autocrine or paracrine receptor activation.
nodes.id: Altered FGFR2 ligand affinity and specificity

evidence.reference_title: Structural basis for fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 activation in Apert syndrome.
evidence.snippet: These structures demonstrate that both mutations introduce additional interactions between FGFR2 and FGF2, thereby augmenting FGFR2-FGF2 affinity.

evidence.reference_title: Structural basis for fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 activation in Apert syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Alterations in FGFR2 ligand affinity and specificity may allow inappropriate autocrine or paracrine activation of FGFR2.

nodes.description: Broad, radially deviated thumbs are characteristic.

evidence.reference_title: The anatomy and management of the thumb in Apert syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The characteristic "hitchhiker" posture or radial clinodactyly of these short but broad digits is caused by an abnormal proximal phalanx.

nodes.description: Bilateral coronal suture fusion causing brachycephaly with turricephaly (tower-shaped skull) and flat occiput.

evidence.reference_title: Apert syndrome results from localized mutations of FGFR2 and is allelic with Crouzon syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Apert syndrome is a distinctive human malformation comprising craniosynostosis and severe syndactyly of the hands and feet.

nodes.description: Apert-mutant FGFR2 activates the ERK1/2 cascade in mesenchymal cells as one branch of the downstream signaling response.

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 promotes osteogenic differentiation in mesenchymal cells via ERK1/2 and protein kinase C signaling.
evidence.snippet: WT and MT FGFR2 induced ERK1/2 but not JNK or PI3K/AKT phosphorylation.

nodes.description: Activated Apert FGFR2 signaling accelerates osteoblast differentiation and matrix mineralization within cranial suture tissues.
nodes.id: Enhanced osteoblast differentiation and matrix mineralization in cranial suture mesenchyme

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 promotes osteogenic differentiation in mesenchymal cells via ERK1/2 and protein kinase C signaling.
evidence.snippet: Both WT and MT FGFR2 increased early and late osteoblast gene expression and matrix mineralization.

nodes.description: Apert-mutant osteoblast-lineage cells show increased constitutive receptor activity together with an FGF2-driven autocrine loop that reinforces aberrant FGFR2 signaling.
nodes.id: FGF2 autocrine loop and constitutive FGFR2 activation

biological_processes: fibroblast growth factor receptor signaling pathway

evidence.reference_title: P253R fibroblast growth factor receptor-2 mutation induces RUNX2 transcript variants and calvarial osteoblast differentiation.
evidence.snippet: FGF2 secretion was greater.

evidence.reference_title: P253R fibroblast growth factor receptor-2 mutation induces RUNX2 transcript variants and calvarial osteoblast differentiation.
evidence.snippet: All together these findings suggest increased constitutive receptor activity in Apert mutant osteoblasts and an autocrine loop involving the FGF2 pathway in modulation of Apert osteoblast behavior.

evidence.reference_title: Apert syndrome results from localized mutations of FGFR2 and is allelic with Crouzon syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We have identified specific missense substitutions involving adjacent amino acids (Ser252Trp and Pro253Arg) in the linker between the second and third extracellular immunoglobulin (Ig) domains of fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 (FGFR2) in all 40 unrelated cases of Apert syndrome studied.

evidence.reference_title: FGFR2 / Apert syndrome (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: FGFR2 | HGNC:3689 | Apert syndrome | MONDO:0007041 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: Apert syndrome is caused almost exclusively by the adjacent FGFR2 S252W and P253R substitutions in the linker between immunoglobulin-like domains II and III, defining a recurrent activating receptor genotype.

evidence.reference_title: Apert syndrome results from localized mutations of FGFR2 and is allelic with Crouzon syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We have identified specific missense substitutions involving adjacent amino acids (Ser252Trp and Pro253Arg) in the linker between the second and third extracellular immunoglobulin (Ig) domains of fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 (FGFR2) in all 40 unrelated cases of Apert syndrome studied.

nodes.description: Aberrant FGFR2 activation disrupts normal appendicular skeletal patterning, producing the characteristic syndactylous hand and foot malformations of Apert syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Soluble form of FGFR2 with S252W partially prevents craniosynostosis of the apert mouse model.
evidence.snippet: Apert syndrome (AS) is characterized by craniosynostosis, midfacial hypoplasia, and bony syndactyly. It is an autosomal dominantly inherited disease caused by point mutations (S252W or P253R) in fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) 2. These mutations cause activation of FGFR2 depending on ligand binding.

nodes.description: Premature cranial suture closure restricts skull-base growth and contributes to the characteristic craniofacial growth pattern of Apert syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment timing and multidisciplinary approach in Apert syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Abnormalities associated with Apert syndrome include premature fusion of coronal sutures system (coronal sutures and less frequently lambdoid suture) resulting in brachiturricephalic dismorphism and impaired skull base growth.

nodes.description: Severe midface hypoplasia with class III malocclusion, contributing to obstructive sleep apnea and feeding difficulties.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment timing and multidisciplinary approach in Apert syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Apert syndrome is a rare congenital disorder characterized by craniosynostosis, midface hypoplasia and symmetric syndactyly of hands and feet.

nodes.description: The Apert-mutant receptor engages a mutant-specific PKCalpha signaling branch that amplifies the osteogenic program beyond wild-type FGFR2 signaling.

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 promotes osteogenic differentiation in mesenchymal cells via ERK1/2 and protein kinase C signaling.
evidence.snippet: MT, but not WT, also increased protein kinase C (PKC) activity.

nodes.description: Excess osteogenic activity in coronal suture mesenchyme causes early closure of the coronal sutures and distorts cranial growth.

evidence.reference_title: Soluble form of FGFR2 with S252W partially prevents craniosynostosis of the apert mouse model.
evidence.snippet: In Ap mice, the coronal suture (CS) was fused prematurely at P1.

nodes.description: Shallow orbits cause ocular proptosis with risk of exposure keratopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Apert's Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Craniofacial deformities include cone-shaped calvarium, fat forehead, prop-tosis, hypertelorism and short nose with a bulbous tip.

nodes.description: Syndactyly of toes, typically involving toes 2-4, creating "sock feet."

evidence.reference_title: Treatment timing and multidisciplinary approach in Apert syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Apert syndrome is a rare congenital disorder characterized by craniosynostosis, midface hypoplasia and symmetric syndactyly of hands and feet.

nodes.description: Symmetric complex syndactyly of digits 2-4 (type I) or 2-5 (type II/III), creating the characteristic "mitten hand" appearance. Both osseous and cutaneous syndactyly are present.

evidence.reference_title: Apert syndrome results from localized mutations of FGFR2 and is allelic with Crouzon syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Apert syndrome is a distinctive human malformation comprising craniosynostosis and severe syndactyly of the hands and feet.

nodes.description: Recurrent pathogenic FGFR2 missense variant in the extracellular linker region that defines the second classic Apert syndrome hotspot allele.
